pages.raw_text:
N. e. Nuzur, associated with the


defense in the M. J. Gordon embez.


Element trial, was the first and only


witness placed upon the stand by


Prosecuting Attorney Pugh this


morning, the cross examination


barely beginning when the morning


session terminated.


Nuzum occupies the peculiar posi-.


ton of counsel for Gordon, when in
| 1908 he was attorney for James S
Sparrow. who secured a judgment
| against the Great Northern railroad
about which the present charge
against the former supreme court
justice and Great Northern attorney
centers.


The expected sensational develop.
ments failed to materialize this
morning, while the interest in the
trial was not apparent by the usual
throng of spectators who usually
crowd the courtroom during the
hearing of an important case. MI
yum testified as to his relations
with Sparrow, his first demand for
$4,000 in payment of fatal injuries
sustained by the young fellow in a
wreck at Great Falls, Mont, in 1907
and the final compromise at $1,000,
which was paid to his brother, E. A.
Nuzum. on August 81, 1908.


In his opening statement Prose-
cutor Pugh outlined the charge con-
tanned in the information, stating
that he intends to prove that the
money to satisfy the claim was paid
over to Gordon on May 26 and ap-
propriated to his own uses, the pay
ment being made on August 81, fol-
lowing the resignation of Gordon as
counsel for the railroad &


GORDON FAILS TO PAY.


In the examination by Prosecutor
Pugh it developed that payment
dragged from week to week, until
NuZum informed Gordon that UNI
less the money was paid early in
August, the matter would be taken
up with Judge Wilson. then in Sc
attic, and judgment secured. The
money was not paid upon the speci-
fled date and on August 6 the judg-
ment was rendered. About August
Is Gordon resigned and on August
RI. following telegram from L.. C.
Gilman of the Seattle branch of the
Great Northern legal department,
the $4,000 was paid to R. A. Nuzum


Attorney Frank Graves attempted
to show upon cross examination
that Gordon was ill at Tacoma at
the time of the receipt of the de-
mand for the settlement.
